title: Projectile Impact Shock-Induced Deformation of One-Component Polymer Nanocomposite Thin Films
abstract: Matrix-free assemblies of polymer-grafted nanoparticles (PGNs) enable mechanically robust materials for a variety of structural, electronic, and optical applications. Recent quasi-static mechanical studies have identified the key parameters that enhance canopy entanglement and promote plasticity of the PGNs below  $T_g$ . Here we experimentally explore the high-strain-rate shock impact behavior of polystyrene grafted NPs and compare their energy absorption capabilities to that of homopolystyrene for film thicknesses ranging from 75 to 550 nm and for impact velocities from 350 to 800 m/s. Modeling reveals that the initial shock compression results in a rapid temperature increase at the impact site. The uniformity of this heating is consistent with observations of greater kinetic energy absorption per mass  $(E_n^*)$  of thinner films due to extensive visco-plastic deformation of molten film around the penetration site. Adiabatic heating is insufficient to raise the temperature at the exit surface of the thickest films resulting in increased strain localization at the impact periphery with less melt elongation. The extent and distribution of entanglements also influence  $E_n^*$ . Structurally, each NP acts as a giant cross-link node, coupling surrounding nodes via the number of canopy chains per NP and the nature and number of entanglements between canopies anchored to different NPs. Load sharing via this dual network, along with geometrical factors such as film thickness, lead to extreme  $E_n^*$  arising from the sequence of instantaneous adiabatic shock heating followed by visco-plastic drawing of the film by the projectile. These observations elucidate the critical factors necessary to create robust polymer-nanocomposite multifunctional films.
results:
In all past LIPIT investigations of thin film perforation,  $^{13-17}$  the role of the initial shock compression on the resultant material behavior has not been taken into account. Here, the early stages of the impact-induced shock compression are modeled and results show good correspondence with the quite different experimentally observed deformation morphologies and respective  $E_p^*$  values for thin vs. thicker films. Experiments on various polystyrene-grafted SiO<sub>2</sub> and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> PGNs films demonstrate the importance of canopy entanglements and NP node cross-links to tailor instantaneous and cumulative volumetric-deformation mechanisms as a function of shock loading and projectile perforation.

PGN structural features and the cross sectional morphology of the thin film PGN assembly are illustrated in Scheme 1. The mechanical behavior can be modified via choice of the nanoparticle core radius  $(r_0)$ , graft density (anchored chains per unit area of the nanoparticle,  $	ext{Σ}$ ), and graft chain length (N), which along with  $r_0$  and  $	ext{Σ}$ , determines the grafted chain conformation and hence the distribution of entanglement density and the number of entanglements per chain. Recent quasi-static uniaxial tensile studies have established the relationship between various PGN designs, intercanopy entanglement, and their quasi-static thin film mechanical behavior below  $T_{m g}^{m 8-12}$  Four types of PS-SiO<sub>2</sub> and PS-Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> PGNs were synthesized by surface-initiated living radical polymerization methods, based on established protocols (see Supporting Information (SI)). The Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and SiO<sub>2</sub> spherical core nanoparticles have  $r_0 = 3.9 	ext{ nm}$  and  $8.0 	ext{ nm}$  grafted with low (unentangled) and high molecular weight (well entangled) chains having graft densities from 0.04 to 0.57 chains/nm<sup>2</sup>, respectively (Table 1). Since the NP core volume fractions in the high molecular weight samples are only 1.3–6.4%, the core NP materials do not greatly affect the bulk modulus. Hence, the material equation of state (EOS) and wave propagation are assumed to be those of homopolystyrene when considering the initial shock response of the film (see hydrocode simulations in the SI).
discussion: The superior performance of SiO<sub>2</sub>-PS231 is consistent with the larger areal damage zone and a more extended melt drawn cone and flaps surrounding the impact site (e.g., Figure 2a1-2a4). Additionally, the impact simulations (Figure 1) imply thinner films facilitate conditions (temperature and stress) around the impact site periphery that would promote melt draw. Furthermore, geometric constraints at the NP surface and interfaces of PGN film assemblies alter canopy entanglements and influence chain mobility, as seen in homopolymers.<sup>21,35–37</sup> Notably, for a monolayer PGN film, canopy entanglements are confined to the interparticle volume within in the film, with essentially no overlap between canopies possible at the film surfaces directly above or below a nanoparticle core.<sup>38</sup> For the 75 nm film, only one SiO<sub>2</sub> PGN spans the film thickness and must lie near the film center, whereas 2-3 span the thickness for the smaller core Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> PGNs (Table 1). The modified PGN interactions in these near-surface regions likely enhance the initial ductility of the film.
conclusion: The variation of the entanglement density throughout the film, the number of entanglements per canopy, and the total length of the tethered chains all positively influence the energy absorbing abilities. Herein, the nodal nanoparticle cross-links in SiO<sub>2</sub>-PS231 apparently result in more polymer draw around the periphery of the impact site due to the larger number of effective entanglements between near-neighbor PGNs than the nodal Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> network in Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>-PS250 comprised of longer chains (250 kg/mol vs. 231 kg/mol) but with smaller cores with many fewer chains grafted per node. However, there is likely a diminishing return for increasing core and canopy size to increase melt strength at these strain rates due to the eventual onset of bond breakage either by scission of entangled chains or by bond fracture at the polymer-NP interface. Thus, the interplay of nanoparticle shape, size, volume fraction, polymer graft density, and polymer entanglement length on local segment mobility, as well as their influence on the number and spatial distribution of entanglements and the nodal nanoparticle network combine together to determine the energy dissipation, melt viscosity and tenacity. Overall, the ability to blend nanoparticle functionality with polymer responsivity under these extreme shock events suggests substantial opportunities for multifunctional protection systems to enhance a wide range of technologies.
